content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Instead of practicing SQL with random datasets, I wanted to work on something I already found interesting. Apple’s Top Songs of 2024: Global chart seemed like a good choice. It only contains twenty songs, but that’s enough to build a relational database and practice writing SQL queries that resemble real-world scenarios.
The goal wasn’t to build a large database. It was to get comfortable with SQL concepts like joins, grouping, CTEs, and window functions using a dataset that was small enough to understand completely. That way, I could verify every query instead of simply trusting the output.
This project ended up teaching me much more than I expected — especially after I made a mistake that looked perfectly correct at first.
Turning a chart into a database
The Top 20 chart looks like a simple list of songs, but once you start asking questions like “Which songs are collaborations?” or “Which genre appears most often?”, it naturally becomes a relational database.
I organized the data into six tables:

• Artists — name, gender, country

• Songs — title, main artist, duration, chart position

• Collabs — featured artists for each song

• Genres — genre information

• ExclusiveContent — explicit songs

• SongFullReport — a view combining all tables
Artists ─┬─< Songs ─┬─< Collabs >─┐
│ ├─< Genres │
│ └─< ExclusiveContent
└────────────────────────┘The schema is fairly simple, but it allowed me to practice writing proper JOINs and answer questions that would be difficult to explore in a spreadsheet.
What I found
After loading the data, I started exploring it with SQL.
Some of the results were:

• 85% of the main artists are from the United States, while the remaining artists come from Canada, Ireland, and Japan.

• 71% of the artists are male and 29% are female. When counting songs instead of artists, the ratio changes to 65% and 35%, since artists like Sabrina Carpenter and Taylor Swift each appear more than once.

• Pop is the most common genre, followed by Hip-Hop and Country.

• Half of the songs contain explicit content.

• 20% of the songs are collaborations.
Before digging into individual SQL queries, I created a simple dashboard to summarize the dataset. It helped me quickly spot patterns such as genre distribution, artist demographics, collaborations, and explicit content.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Dashboard generated using Python (Pandas and Matplotlib) from the SQL query results.
The bug that taught me the most
The collaboration percentage was the most interesting result — not because of the number itself, but because my first query produced the wrong answer.
My initial query was:
SELECT COUNT(*) * 100.0 /
(SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Songs) AS CollabPercentage
FROM Collabs;It looked completely reasonable.
The problem was that COUNT(*) counts rows, not songs. One song, "Like That," has two featured artists, so it appears twice in the Collabs table. As a result, the query counted that song twice and returned 25% instead of 20%.
The fix was surprisingly small:
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT SongID) * 100.0 /
(SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Songs) AS CollabPercentage
FROM Collabs;Using COUNT(DISTINCT SongID) counts unique songs instead of rows.
It’s the kind of mistake SQL won’t warn you about. The query runs successfully, the result looks believable, and unless you double-check the data, it’s easy to accept an incorrect number. Fixing that single line taught me more than several tutorial examples.
Finally understanding window functions
Another concept I wanted to understand better was window functions.
One query ranked songs within each genre:
WITH RankedByGenre AS (
SELECT
g.Genre,
s.Title,
s.AppleChartsPosition,
RANK() OVER (
PARTITION BY g.Genre
ORDER BY s.AppleChartsPosition
) AS GenreRank
FROM Genres g
JOIN Songs s
ON g.SongID = s.SongID
)
SELECT Genre, Title, AppleChartsPosition
FROM RankedByGenre
WHERE GenreRank = 1
ORDER BY AppleChartsPosition;This was the query that finally made window functions click for me.
PARTITION BY Genre creates separate rankings for each genre, allowing me to identify the highest-charting Pop song, Hip-Hop song, Country song, and so on without using multiple subqueries or self-joins.
I also used window functions to calculate the cumulative percentage of explicit songs across the chart, which was a nice way to visualize how the ratio changes as you move through the rankings.
Project repository
The complete project — including the database schema, sample data, SQL queries, and the Python script used to generate the visualizations — is available on GitHub.
🔗 https://github.com/sumeyyatas/apple-charts-2024-sql-analysis
To recreate the project:
mysql -u root -p < topsongs_2024_v2.sql
pip install pandas matplotlib
python3 generate_charts.pyFinal thoughts
This isn’t a big dataset, and that was exactly the point.
Because I understood every row, it was easy to question unexpected results and figure out why they happened. On a much larger dataset, that collaboration bug could have gone unnoticed for a long time.
Working on a small project like this reminded me that learning SQL isn’t only about writing queries that run — it’s about understanding whether the answers they return actually make sense.
Sometimes the best way to learn a tool is to use it on data you can fully understand and let your mistakes teach you something new.
